key_insight: A neural network can replace the expensive constitutive update in plasticity by directly predicting state-variable evolution while still enforcing the plastic consistency condition exactly.
engineering_relevance: This can substantially speed up finite element simulations of nonlinear materials, especially when large time steps are needed without sacrificing stability or accuracy.
method: Train neural networks on invariants from 10 cycles of multiaxial proportional loading data.
Use analytical rules to determine the required evolution directions from the training data.
Embed the learned time integrator into a von Mises plasticity model with nonlinear kinematic hardening and evaluate it in material-point and finite-element simulations.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports strong accuracy, numerical stability, and noticeable computational savings in both material-point tests and finite-element boundary value simulations.
watch_item: Performance is demonstrated on a prototype model and limited loading data, so broader validation is still needed for more complex loading paths and material behaviors.
paper_title: Machine Learning-Accelerated Time Integration of Plasticity Models
